activityOfUser.occupation: Principal Software Engineer Manager at Microsoft

posts.text:
The competition for Day 0 or even Day -1 slots in Indian campus recruitment is becoming intense, bordering on craziness.
Companies want to secure slots earlier than others and are offering incentives to colleges to do so. Colleges, in turn, are looking to capitalize on this situation by improving their recruitment records or securing sponsorships for college fests.
However, in this process, two important participants—the interviewers and the candidates—are being blatantly ignored. Recruitment is starting at midnight, often going into the early hours of the morning. Despite all recommendations and research, companies expect interviewers and candidates to make sharp decisions at 2 or 3 a.m., when they are sleep-deprived.
What would be lost if interviews started in the morning? Why have we come to accept this as normal?

comments.text: Valid point, sounds like the players have found Nash equilibrium.

posts.text:
'Joy of Reading' needs your help

“Reading opens an infinite world of ideas and sparks imagination in children all over the world. Gift a book and spread the joy of reading.”

This year we are enrolled in ‘Benevity Causes Portal’ which enables donations through your company’s giving portals. During this giving month, I request you to consider donating to 'Joy of Reading' and help us spread the Joy and fulfill our mission of “Enabling every child to immerse in the world of Books."

More about our work: At Joy of Reading (https://joyofreading.org/), we tailor our contributions to the specific needs of government schools. By supplying books that cater to the unique demographics of each school, we ensure that every child has access to literature that resonates with them. Beyond just providing books, we share our expertise on library setup, maintenance, and management. We also train librarians on their pivotal roles and effective methods to foster reading among children. Additionally, we offer insights on conducting children's reading level assessments, advise on executing a structured library hour, guide English teachers on utilizing the library as a teaching resource, and provide strategies to support struggling readers. Through these efforts, we empower government school teachers to manage libraries efficiently, cultivate reading habits in children, and assist those who face reading challenges.
Read about our recent projects and impact: https://lnkd.in/g2QKMByE 
More donation options:
Cash Donations: https://lnkd.in/gncUFmfA 
Donate books: https://lnkd.in/geGp89-i  
Give.do: https://r.give.do/KV7Rj88o

Called me twice today about it. Why are you pestering people about it? Might as well get rid of HDFC credit cards & account due to the insane amount of marketing calls. resharedPost.text:
As an international student who came to the US in 2008 and who benefited from the quality of education here, I have an advice for those who were thinking of coming to US for higher education any time soon (this year and 2021) - DO NOT come to the US for your higher education. There are 2 reasons for this that are straightforward:

(1) Quality of education on any online tool is subpar as compared to the on campus experience. Most classes are now being conducted online. I will be honest - it’s not worth paying the kind of money international students pay in tuition. There is no question that US has some of the best faculty but still the quality of online education would make you question what you are paying for. Several US higher education institutions today have impressive buildings and campuses, in many cases funded by higher tuition charged to international students. But, if you are not able to get any benefit from it, then what’s the point. 

(2) Several companies in US are not willing to hire international students with visa requirements. It’s likely there will be more visa restrictions imposed in this economy which will also limit your ability to find an internship or job. If you are paying upwards of 50000$ for a degree, you need to have a plan to pay that massive loan.

comments.text: Mukul Aggarwal this makes a lot of sense to me.

But I guess the education is still there, depending on how a lecturer plans to do it, the students could still learn as much especially with the ability to re-watch video lectures over and over again.

author.occupation: Utility Systems Specialist Lead | Project Coordinator | MS in Global Business and Trade at OSU |

comments.text:
Well what you said might be true for the short term as you mentioned (Fall 2020 or Spring 2021) especially about the Online classes and fee being paid for that..

But the situation about job opportunity might completely change either in Nov 2020, or even after. Currently there is only a suspension in visa's (limited time) due to this very high unemployment rate, but it is not a cancellation of visas. 

And considering the stand what US has taken which is Economy over Pandemic, there is a good possibility of the Economy getting back soon than anywhere else. Just for considering as a point, the loss of lives will also add to demand for talent in future (deepest condolences for people and family that lost lives). The major hit would be for startup companies, but the government did provide them some aid in recovery or to stay afloat. 

So the economy will survive, just the current situation is bad and doesnt mean will be same in future. And it is not going to take another 1.5-2 years for this Economy to get back (normal timeline for an International student in completing Masters). So I would say this is the good time to visit US as in the next 1.5 years or so there would be a lot of opportunities. - Comments?

posts.text:
As an international student who came to the US in 2008 and who benefited from the quality of education here, I have an advice for those who were thinking of coming to US for higher education any time soon (this year and 2021) - DO NOT come to the US for your higher education. There are 2 reasons for this that are straightforward:

(1) Quality of education on any online tool is subpar as compared to the on campus experience. Most classes are now being conducted online. I will be honest - it’s not worth paying the kind of money international students pay in tuition. There is no question that US has some of the best faculty but still the quality of online education would make you question what you are paying for. Several US higher education institutions today have impressive buildings and campuses, in many cases funded by higher tuition charged to international students. But, if you are not able to get any benefit from it, then what’s the point. 

(2) Several companies in US are not willing to hire international students with visa requirements. It’s likely there will be more visa restrictions imposed in this economy which will also limit your ability to find an internship or job. If you are paying upwards of 50000$ for a degree, you need to have a plan to pay that massive loan.
